message.content:
CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). ROUND 2 for T89 (goals:G4) — "Gate DATA/PARITY from unbound sources".
Round-1 reconciled disapprove carried ONE criticism: the PARITY subtest was non-discriminating under the FEC-off fixture (a leaked PARITY dropped at a nil fecRecv regardless of the gate). Worker fixed it in 7900642 (implement/T89 now at 7900642; round-2 diff = e876ba0..7900642, TEST-ONLY): arms receive-side FEC on BOTH peers via the Open seam (Store a fecReceiver), feeds a DataCount=1 parity whose sole data shard reconstructs into the dispatching peer's resequencer on a leak — observable via the decoder Recovered counter + resequencer Pop; re-verified discriminating by mutation (the dispatch-without-bind leak now fails the PARITY subtest too).
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a60e7a44eaa038405 (branch implement/T89, base 352c1e5). Go repo — gate incl. `go test -race ./internal/bind/...` via nix develop.
Judge whether the criticism is resolved (the PARITY subtest now discriminates a gate leak). Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"testing"
6		"time"
7	
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
11	)
12	
13	// fecReceiveConfig is the fixed-ratio group the strengthened PARITY subtest arms both
14	// peers' receivers with. K=1 parity is all that is needed: a DataCount=1 group (one data
15	// shard) reconstructs that sole data frame from the single parity shard ALONE, so a PARITY
16	// that leaks past the demux gate into dispatchInbound recovers a data frame straight into
17	// the dispatching peer's resequencer — which is what makes the leak observable.
18	var fecReceiveConfig = fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: time.Millisecond}
19	
20	// enableFECReceive arms a peer's receive-side FEC through the SAME seam Open uses
21	// (openPeerDatapathLocked, multipath.go:851) to Store a fecReceiver. Without it the Parity
22	// case of dispatchInbound is a no-op (fecRecv nil), so a leaked PARITY would touch no
23	// resequencer and the drop property this subtest names would be unobservable.
24	func enableFECReceive(t testing.TB, p *peerState, cfg fec.Config) {
25		t.Helper()
26		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(cfg)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build FEC decoder: %v", err)
29		}
30		p.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
31	}
32	
33	// singleFrameParity builds a GENUINE parity shard for a one-data-frame group whose sole
34	// data shard's coded bytes are seq||inner (fecShardPayload) — the exact shape the datapath
35	// codes parity over. Offered to a decoder built at the same cfg, this parity ALONE
36	// reconstructs that data frame, so a PARITY carrying it would deliver seq||inner up a
37	// resequencer at outer-seq `seq`. It returns the wire fields for a frame.Parity.
38	func singleFrameParity(t testing.TB, cfg fec.Config, seq uint64, inner []byte) (group uint32, index uint16, dataCount uint8, payload []byte) {
39		t.Helper()
40		enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(cfg, fec.SystemClock{})
41		if err != nil {
42			t.Fatalf("build FEC encoder: %v", err)
43		}
44		if _, _, aerr := enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, inner)); aerr != nil {
45			t.Fatalf("admit single data shard: %v", aerr)
46		}
47		parity, err := enc.Flush() // close the 1-data group, coding K parity over its sole shard
48		if err != nil {
49			t.Fatalf("flush single-frame group: %v", err)
50		}
51		if len(parity) != cfg.ParityShards {
52			t.Fatalf("single-frame group emitted %d parity, want %d", len(parity), cfg.ParityShards)
53		}
54		pshard := parity[0]
55		if pshard.DataCount != 1 {
56			t.Fatalf("single-frame parity DataCount = %d, want 1", pshard.DataCount)
57		}
58		return uint32(pshard.Group), uint16(pshard.Index), uint8(pshard.DataCount), pshard.Payload
59	}
60	
61	// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
62	// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
63	// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
64	// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
65	// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
66	// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
67	// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
68	// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
69	//
70	// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
71	// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
72	// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
73	func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
74		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
75		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
76	
77		// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
78		// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
79		// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
80		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
81		if err != nil {
82			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
83		}
84	
85		t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
86			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
87			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
88			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
89	
90			data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
91			if err != nil {
92				t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
93			}
94			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
95	
96			// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
97			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
98				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
99			}
100			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
101				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
102			}
103			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
104				t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
105			}
106		})
107	
108		t.Run("PARITY that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
109			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
110			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
111			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
112	
113			// Arm BOTH peers' receive-side FEC exactly as Open does (multipath.go:851). This is
114			// what makes the drop OBSERVABLE: with FEC receive off (the prior fixture), a PARITY
115			// leaked past the gate falls into dispatchInbound's fecRecv-nil no-op and touches no
116			// resequencer, so the subtest could not distinguish a gate that drops from one that
117			// dispatches. With FEC receive on, the DataCount=1 parity below reconstructs its sole
118			// data frame straight into the DISPATCHING peer's resequencer, so a leak is caught by
119			// both the decoder-recovery and the resequencer assertions (mutation-verified).
120			enableFECReceive(t, primary, fecReceiveConfig)
121			enableFECReceive(t, second, fecReceiveConfig)
122	
123			// A GENUINE single-frame-group parity: its one data shard's coded bytes are
124			// leakSeq||leakInner, so if this PARITY were dispatched into either peer's decoder it
125			// alone would recover that data frame and resequence leakInner at outer-seq leakSeq.
126			const leakSeq = 42
127			leakInner := []byte("parity-leak-recovers-this")
128			group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, fecReceiveConfig, leakSeq, leakInner)
129	
130			parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: parityPayload})
131			if err != nil {
132				t.Fatalf("encode PARITY under psk B: %v", err)
133			}
134			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, peerAP)
135	
136			// The gate dropped the unbound PARITY BEFORE dispatch: it reached NEITHER peer's FEC
137			// decoder (no reconstruction) NOR any resequencer. A dispatch-without-bind leak would
138			// trip whichever peer the leaked frame was dispatched to, so both are asserted.
139			for _, pc := range []struct {
140				who string
141				p   *peerState
142			}{{"peer B", second}, {"the PRIMARY", primary}} {
143				fr := pc.p.fecRecv.Load()
144				if got := fr.stats().Recovered; got != 0 {
145					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY reached %s's FEC decoder and reconstructed %d frame(s); a valid decode carries no binding authority and must not be dispatched", pc.who, got)
146				}
147				if got := fr.deliveredRecovered.Load(); got != 0 {
148					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY delivered %d reconstructed frame(s) up %s's resequencer", got, pc.who)
149				}
150				if it, ok := pc.p.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
151					t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in %s's resequencer (%q)", pc.who, it.Payload)
152				}
153			}
154			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
155				t.Fatal("an unbound PARITY frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
156			}
157		})
158	
159		t.Run("after an authenticated PROBE binds the source to B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only", func(t *testing.T) {
160			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
161			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
162			peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
163	
164			// Pre-binding DATA under B's psk from this source is dropped (the gate above).
165			early, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 4, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("early")})
166			if err != nil {
167				t.Fatalf("encode pre-bind DATA: %v", err)
168			}
169			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], early, peerAP)
170			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
171				t.Fatal("pre-bind DATA established a binding")
172			}
173	
174			// An authenticated PROBE under peer B's psk binds the source to peer B.
175			const seq = 7
176			ts := newFakeClock().Now().UnixNano()
177			probeRaw, err := frame.Encode(pskB, frame.Probe{PathID: secondView.id, ProbeSeq: seq, TimestampNanos: ts, IsEcho: false})
178			if err != nil {
179				t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk B: %v", err)
180			}
181			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeRaw, peerAP)
182	
183			bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr())
184			if !ok || bound != second {
185				t.Fatalf("PROBE did not bind the source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
186			}
187			// Drain peer B's reflected echo so a later timeout-read in the suite is unambiguous;
188			// its content is asserted by the T88 acceptance, so we only consume it here.
189			echoCodec, _ := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
190			_ = readProbe(t, peer, echoCodec)
191	
192			// Subsequent DATA from the now-bound source lands in peer B's resequencer, and ONLY B's.
193			late, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 9, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("late")})
194			if err != nil {
195				t.Fatalf("encode post-bind DATA: %v", err)
196			}
197			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], late, peerAP)
198			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("late")) {
199				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA did not reach peer B's resequencer: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
200			}
201			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
202				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA for peer B leaked into the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
203			}
204			// The pre-bind "early" frame was never buffered anywhere: peer B's resequencer held
205			// only "late" (already popped) and the primary's held nothing.
206			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
207				t.Fatalf("peer B's resequencer held a second frame (%q); the pre-bind DATA was not dropped", it.Payload)
208			}
209		})
210	}
211

content.content:
100		lastControlTick time.Time
101		haveControlTick bool
102	
103		dataFrames   atomic.Uint64
104		dataBytes    atomic.Uint64
105		parityFrames atomic.Uint64
106		parityBytes  atomic.Uint64
107	}
108	
109	// fecReceiver is the receive-side FEC state: the recovery decoder guarded by its own
110	// mutex (the per-path readLoop goroutines feed it concurrently). deliveredRecovered is
111	// the HONEST recovery count for /metrics — data frames a reconstruction actually
112	// delivered to the resequencer AHEAD of its release point (not merely reconstructed:
113	// a frame rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and
114	// must not be counted, else /metrics overstates recovery). It is an atomic so the
115	// snapshot reads it lock-free.
116	type fecReceiver struct {
117		mu  sync.Mutex
118		dec *fec.Decoder
119	
120		deliveredRecovered atomic.Uint64
121	
122		// connLoss is the POST-FEC-RECOVERY residual-loss estimator (T29, the P4 signal):
123		// every natively-received DATA outer-seq AND every FEC-reconstructed outer-seq is
124		// Observed into it, so its Loss() fraction is exactly the connection-scoped loss that
125		// survived FEC — outer-seqs neither received nor recovered. It is internally
126		// synchronized (its own mutex), fed concurrently by the per-path readLoop goroutines
127		// exactly like the resequencer, and read lock-free-enough at scrape time.
128		connLoss *telemetry.ConnLoss
129	}
130	
131	// offer feeds one surviving shard into the decoder and returns any data payloads it
132	// reconstructed (never-received frames). It holds the decoder mutex only for the
133	// in-memory reconstruct — never across a syscall — so it never blocks the send path.
134	func (r *fecReceiver) offer(s fec.Shard) ([]fec.Recovered, error) {
135		r.mu.Lock()
136		defer r.mu.Unlock()
137		return r.dec.Offer(s)
138	}
139	
140	// stats snapshots the decoder's cumulative recovery counters under its mutex.
141	func (r *fecReceiver) stats() fec.DecoderStats {
142		r.mu.Lock()
143		defer r.mu.Unlock()
144		return r.dec.Stats()
145	}
146	
147	// FECStats is a consistent snapshot of the Bind's FEC counters (T24), the shape the
148	// metrics.Source adapter maps onto the connection-scoped /metrics FEC series.
149	// DataFrames is the send-side DATA-frame count (the fixed-ratio overhead's
150	// denominator); ParityFrames/ParityBytes are the send-side overhead (parity emitted);
151	// Recovered and Unrecoverable are the receive-side outcome (data shards reconstructed
152	// from parity, and data shards lost beyond repair capacity). FEC being
153	// connection-scoped, these carry no path label. The frame overhead the operator cares
154	// about is ParityFrames/DataFrames, which tends to ParityShards/DataShards (M/K) once
155	// groups fill; both are counted only for frames that actually reached the socket, so
156	// the ratio reflects wire cost actually spent.
157	type FECStats struct {
158		DataFrames    uint64
159		DataBytes     uint64
160		ParityFrames  uint64
161		ParityBytes   uint64
162		Recovered     uint64
163		Unrecoverable uint64
164		// ResidualLoss is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
165		// (T29): the share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from
166		// parity. It is the P4 acceptance signal — the loss FEC did not mask. Zero when FEC is
167		// disabled or no data has flowed.
168		ResidualLoss float64
169	}
170	
171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))
177		binary.BigEndian.PutUint64(b, seq)
178		copy(b[fecSeqPrefixLen:], inner)
179		return b
180	}
181	
182	// splitFECShardPayload inverts fecShardPayload, returning the outer-seq and a fresh
183	// copy of the inner datagram. It fails fast on a shard too short to hold the prefix,
184	// which would indicate a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable
185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {
188		if len(b) < fecSeqPrefixLen {
189			return 0, nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: recovered FEC shard %d bytes, shorter than the %d-byte outer-seq prefix", len(b), fecSeqPrefixLen)

1222	// dispatchInbound handles one already-decoded inbound frame on the resolved peer's view (ps):
1223	// it routes to that peer's resequencer / FEC decoder / reflector. The source demux in
1224	// demuxInbound has already selected ps so a shared socket serving many peers resequences each
1225	// peer's stream against that peer's own buffer; on the single-peer edge/hub ps.peer is the
1226	// embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton. raw is retained for
1227	// the probe transport (HandleEcho / Reflect re-decode it under the peer's psk).
1228	func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1229		pr := ps.peer
1230		switch f := fr.(type) {
1231		case frame.Data:
1232			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1233			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1234			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1235			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1236				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1237				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1238				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1239				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1240				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1241				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1242				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1243				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1244				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1245				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1246				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1247				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1248					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1249				}
1250				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1251			} else {
1252				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1253			}
1254		case frame.Parity:
1255			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1256			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1257			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1258			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1259			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1260			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1261				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1262				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1263				m.observeRecovered(fr, pr.resequencer.Load(), recovered, srcAP)
1264			}
1265		case frame.Probe:
1266			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1267			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1268			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1269			if f.IsEcho {
1270				if ps.prober != nil {
1271					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1272					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1273					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1274					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1275					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1276				}
1277				return
1278			}
1279			if pr.reflector != nil {
1280				if echo, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
1281					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
1282					// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
1283					_, _ = ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP)
1284				}
1285			}
1286		default:
1287			// CONTROL (and any unhandled kind) is not delivered up this path.
1288		}
1289	}
1290	
1291	// observeRecovered feeds every FEC-reconstructed data frame into the resequencer at
1292	// its ORIGINAL outer-seq (recovered from the shard's coded bytes) via the NON-resyncing
1293	// ObserveRecovered path, so a frame rebuilt from parity resequences identically to a
1294	// natively-received frame — filling the exact gap the resequencer would otherwise time
1295	// out on — WITHOUT a late batch of recovered seqs being able to move the release point
1296	// or dump the live buffer (recovery must never cause loss). Only frames actually placed
1297	// AHEAD of the release point advance the honest delivered-recovery counter; a frame
1298	// rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and not
1299	// counted, so /metrics reflects delivered recovery, not mere reconstruction. A
1300	// malformed recovered shard (too short to hold the outer-seq prefix) is dropped: it
1301	// signals an encoder/decoder mismatch, not a deliverable frame.
1302	func (m *Multipath) observeRecovered(fr *fecReceiver, rq *reseq.Resequencer, recovered []fec.Recovered, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1303		for _, rec := range recovered {
1304			seq, inner, err := splitFECShardPayload(rec.Payload)
1305			if err != nil {
1306				continue
1307			}
1308			// Residual-loss accounting (T29): FEC reconstructed this outer-seq, so it is NOT
1309			// residual loss — mark it present in the post-recovery estimator even if the
1310			// resequencer later drops it as late (that is a latency outcome, not a masking
1311			// failure; the P4 residual bound measures loss FEC failed to mask).
1312			if fr.connLoss != nil {
1313				fr.connLoss.Observe(seq)
1314			}
1315			if rq.ObserveRecovered(seq, inner, srcAP) {
1316				fr.deliveredRecovered.Add(1)
1317			}
1318		}
1319	}
1320	
1321	// virtualEndpoint returns the single stable endpoint the engine holds for the GIVEN
1322	// peer (ps). Each peer owns its OWN virtual endpoint (invariant A1: one virtual endpoint
1323	// per peer), so the drainer stamps a delivered inner datagram with the endpoint of the
1324	// peer whose resequencer released it — the engine thus attributes return traffic to the
1325	// right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's virt. On a peer with no
1326	// configured endpoint (the concentrator) its destination is pinned ONCE to the first
1327	// learned source; thereafter every path returns the identical pointer so the engine sees
1328	// one peer, never per-packet churn.
1329	//
1330	// Hot-path note: the destination, once pinned, never changes, and it is published
1331	// through an atomic.Pointer (see udpEndpoint). So the common case takes a
1332	// lock-free fast path — every received datagram would otherwise contend m.mu with
1333	// in-flight Sends. The mutex is acquired only to pin the FIRST learned source.
1334	func (m *Multipath) virtualEndpoint(ps *peerState, learned netip.AddrPort) Endpoint {
1335		if ps.virt.dstValid() {
1336			return ps.virt
1337		}
1338		m.mu.Lock()
1339		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1340		if !ps.virt.dstValid() {
1341			ps.virt.setDst(learned)

1100	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
1101	//
1102	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
1103	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
1104	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1105	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1106	// natively-received frame.
1107	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1108		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1109		if err != nil {
1110			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1111		}
1112		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1113	}
1114	
1115	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1116	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
1117	// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
1118	// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
1119	// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
1120	//
1121	// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
1122	// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
1123	// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
1124	// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
1125	//
1126	// Shared socket (>1 view):
1127	//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
1128	//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
1129	//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
1130	//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
1131	//     bounded by the static peer count). Only an authenticated PROBE establishes a binding
1132	//     (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned only from authenticated PROBEs), and a
1133	//     PROBE's MAC verifies under EXACTLY ONE psk — so the FIRST psk whose codec yields a PROBE
1134	//     identifies the peer, binds the source, dispatches, and the loop STOPS there. A non-PROBE
1135	//     decode does NOT stop the trial: DATA/PARITY carry no MAC and are forgeable by design, so
1136	//     a genuine PROBE from a later peer can cross-psk-garble into a DATA/PARITY kind under an
1137	//     earlier peer's codec (~0.4%); the loop must therefore `continue` past a non-PROBE decode
1138	//     to give every remaining psk its chance to authenticate a PROBE. A non-PROBE decode
1139	//     carries no binding authority and is dropped either way — a genuine DATA/PARITY from a
1140	//     not-yet-bound source never dispatches or binds until that peer's PROBE binds it, so
1141	//     continuing past it changes nothing for genuine frames. A forged/garbage frame verifies
1142	//     as a PROBE under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
1143	func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1144		views := ps.views.Load()
1145		if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
1146			m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
1147			return
1148		}
1149		if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
1150			for _, v := range *views {
1151				if v.peer == bound {
1152					m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
1153					return
1154				}
1155			}
1156			return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
1157		}
1158		for _, v := range *views {
1159			fr, err := v.codec.Decode(raw)
1160			if err != nil {
1161				continue // not this peer's psk — try the next
1162			}
1163			if _, isProbe := fr.(frame.Probe); !isProbe {
1164				// Decoded under this psk but not a PROBE (DATA/PARITY carry no MAC, so
1165				// this may be a genuine PROBE from a later peer that cross-garbled into an
1166				// unauthenticated kind here). No binding authority: try the remaining psks
1167				// rather than aborting — a genuine unbound DATA/PARITY still never binds.
1168				continue
1169			}
1170			m.bindSourceToPeer(srcAP.Addr(), v.peer)
1171			m.dispatchInbound(v, fr, raw, srcAP) // already decoded: dispatch without re-decoding
1172			return
1173		}
1174		// No peer's psk verified: a forged/garbage frame. No binding, drop.

content.text: The mechanism is sound. Now let me run the test and verify the mutation-discrimination claim.

content.text: Test passes. Now the decisive check — mutate the gate to dispatch a non-PROBE decode (the leak) and confirm the PARITY subtest now fails.

input.description: Mutate demux gate to leak non-PROBE decodes

input.command: cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a60e7a44eaa038405 && timeout 480 nix develop -c go test -race -run TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity -count=1 -v ./internal/bind/ 2>&1 | tail -30; echo "=== RESTORE ==="; cp /tmp/claude-1000/-home-pavel-work-safe-fecalia/671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7/scratchpad/multipath.go.bak internal/bind/multipath.go && git diff --stat

content.text:
All checks green. The round-1 criticism is fully resolved.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T89 round 2 (diff e876ba0..7900642, TEST-ONLY, one file, +86 lines)
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** The PARITY subtest is now discriminating. It arms receive-side FEC on BOTH peers via `enableFECReceive` — an exact replica of Open's seam (`fecReceiver{dec, connLoss: NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)}`, multipath.go:851) — and feeds a genuine `DataCount=1` parity built by `singleFrameParity` whose sole data shard alone reconstructs `leakSeq||leakInner`. A leaked PARITY reaching `dispatchInbound` (multipath.go:1254-1264) would trip the decoder-`Recovered`, `deliveredRecovered`, and resequencer-`Pop` assertions. Verified operationally: `go test -race -run TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity` PASS on HEAD; mutation of the demux gate (multipath.go:1163, dispatch the non-PROBE decode instead of `continue`) makes the PARITY subtest FAIL at L145 ("unbound PARITY reached peer B's FEC decoder and reconstructed 1 frame(s)"). Tree restored clean; `go vet` + full `-race ./internal/bind/...` green.
- **Issues:** none

Mutation-verified: leaking the non-PROBE decode fails the PARITY subtest at L145. Test passes under -race; go vet + full bind package green; tree clean.",
